did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a seven-layer Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with a softmax output layer for the multi-class classification of four skin diseases (Acne Rosacea, Actinic Keratosis, Atopic Dermatitis, and Bullous Disease). The model was trained on 2,800 images and achieved 98% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a seven-layer Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for the multi-class classification of four skin diseases. It reports specific results, including 98% accuracy, AUC scores, and F1 scores, derived from training on 2,800 images and testing on 830 images. Although it contains a literature review section, its primary purpose is to report new technical results from a proposed deep learning model.

title: Dermal Diagnostics: Unveiling the Hidden Language of the Skin
journal: 2025 2nd International Conference on Software, Systems and Information Technology (SSITCON)
brief_summary: This study developed a seven-layer Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with a softmax output layer for the multi-class classification of four skin diseases: Acne Rosacea, Actinic Keratosis, Atopic Dermatitis, and Bullous Disease. The model was trained and validated using a publicly sourced dataset partitioned into 2,800 training images and 830 testing images. Evaluation metrics included classification accuracy, Area Under the Curve (AUC) scores derived from ROC curves, and F1 scores. The proposed model achieved a remarkable overall classification accuracy of 98%, with AUC scores ranging from 0.751 to 0.916 across the different conditions. These results demonstrate the model's high precision and robust capability as a reliable tool for computer-aided dermatological diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the four distinct skin diseases classified by the proposed CNN model. Included in the dataset with 800 training and 800 testing images. Achieved an AUC of 0.916 and an F1 score of 0.92.

all_task_types.target: Four distinct skin disease categories: Acne Rosacea, Actinic Keratosis, Atopic Dermatitis, and Bullous Disease.
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification using a seven-layer Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with a softmax output layer. The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels for skin diseases. Evaluation metrics include classification accuracy (98%), AUC scores (0.751-0.916), and F1 scores (0.91-0.94).

datasets_used.name: Publicly sourced dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset described as 'publicly sourced' containing images of four skin diseases. The specific origin or repository name is not provided in the text, but it is distinct from the external benchmarks (CenterNet, MobileNet, etc.) used for comparison in Table I.

use.training: Used to train the seven-layer CNN model (2,800 images).
use.testing: Used to test the model's performance (830 images).

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Confusion Matrix (True Positives, True Negatives, False Positives, False Negatives)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate the Area Under the Curve (AUC) scores for evaluating diagnostic performance across different skin disease classes.
To assess classification performance by deriving True Positives, True Negatives, False Positives, and False Negatives, which are used to calculate Precision, Recall, and F1 Score.
To measure the error between predicted probabilities and true labels during the training optimization process.
To summarize the overall performance of the classification model by calculating the ratio of accurate predictions to total predictions.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using classification accuracy, AUC scores, and F1 scores.

Publicly sourced dataset (4-class skin d.dataset_description: Dataset partitioned into 2,800 training images and 830 testing images across four classes: Acne Rosacea (AR), Actinic Keratosis (AK), Atopic Dermatitis (AD), and Bullous Disease (BD).
Publicly sourced dataset (4-class skin d.experiment: Multi-class classification using a seven-layer Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with softmax output layer, Adam optimizer, categorical cross-entropy loss, batch size of 10, learning rate of 0.001, over 50 epochs.
Publicly sourced dataset (4-class skin d.notes: The proposed model achieved an overall accuracy of 98%, outperforming the listed baselines. The model was validated using ROC curves and confusion matrices.

architecture_details.total_layers: 7 layers (described as eight layers in one section, but seven in abstract and methodology)
architecture_details.details: The architecture includes convolutional layers with 2D convolution, max pooling for downsampling, dropout to avoid overfitting, flattening of 2D output to a linear vector, and dense fully connected layers. The final layer is a softmax output layer for multi-class classification.

conclusions: The proposed seven-layer Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) achieved a high overall classification accuracy of 98% for the multi-class classification of four skin diseases: Acne Rosacea, Actinic Keratosis, Atopic Dermatitis, and Bullous Disease.
The model demonstrated robust diagnostic performance with Area Under the Curve (AUC) scores ranging from 0.751 to 0.916 across the different disease classes.
The model exhibited exceptional and consistent F1 scores (0.91–0.94), indicating a strong balance between precision and recall for each condition.
The proposed CNN model outperformed several baseline methods, including MobileNet-v2 (97.50%), Localization deep learning (90.91%), CenterNet (83.00%), and a previous deep learning model (66.67%).
The study confirms the model's high precision and robust capability as a reliable tool for computer-aided dermatological diagnosis.

The research addresses limitations in previous works, such as the inability to detect small or irregular lesions (CenterNet), imbalanced grade distribution (Deep learning model [6]), and data sparsity (MobileNet-v2), by proposing a customized CNN architecture.
important_remarks: The dataset is described only as 'publicly sourced' without specifying the repository, origin, or licensing, raising concerns about reproducibility and data provenance.

The total number of testing images (800+1100+500+400 = 2800) contradicts the abstract and methodology which state 830 testing images, indicating a significant inconsistency in reported data partitioning.
The paper claims a seven-layer architecture in the abstract but describes an eight-layer architecture in the methodology section, showing internal inconsistency in model description.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely on the internal test set, limiting the generalizability of the 98% accuracy claim.
The study lacks formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) to support the claim that the proposed model significantly outperforms baselines like MobileNet-v2.

The dataset appears to be highly balanced (or artificially constructed) with equal training and testing counts per class, which may not reflect real-world clinical data distribution and could lead to overoptimistic performance estimates.
No data augmentation techniques were reported, which is unusual for deep learning dermatology studies and may contribute to potential overfitting despite the use of dropout.

The comparison with baseline models (CenterNet, MobileNet-v2, etc.) uses accuracy metrics from previous studies without re-evaluating them on the same dataset, making the performance comparison potentially invalid due to dataset mismatch.
